It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.

For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 12 of about 42 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking at (28, 33) inside the blob didn't move any squares! The M squares are all in the same positions. So clicking on the blue blob doesn't move squares. This means the click only affects squares that are "close enough" or perhaps only cells that are adjacent to or part of the square.
Let me reconsider. Looking at the successful moves:
1. Click (36, 17) on square at (36-37, 17-18) → moved to (35-36, 16-17) [clicked cell is within the square]
2. Click (36, 41) on square at (36-37, 40-41) → moved to (35-36, 40-41) [clicked cell is within the square]
3. Click (34, 16) on square at (35-36, 16-17) → moved to (33-34, 15-16) [clicked cell is 1 row above the square]
And the failed move:
4. Click (28, 33) near blob → no square moved [clicked cell is far from all squares]
So there seems to be a range limit. The click only moves a square if the clicked cell is within some distance. Let me figure out the range. For case 3, the clicked cell (34, 16) is adjacent to the square (35-36, 16-17) - it's one cell above the top-left corner.
Maybe the rule is: clicking a cell within 1 cell of the square (including the square itself) moves the square so the clicked cell becomes the bottom-right corner.
Let me test: click at (35, 14) which is 2 cells to the left of square 1 at (33-34, 15-16). If the range is 1, this shouldn't move the square.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the mechanic more carefully. Looking at case 3:
- Square was at (35-36, 16-17)
- Clicked (34, 16)
- Square moved to (33-34, 15-16)
The clicked cell (34, 16) is the bottom-right of the NEW square position. But (34, 16) was 1 cell above the original square's top-left corner (35, 16).
So the rule might be: if you click a cell that's within the square OR adjacent to it (including diagonally), the square moves so the clicked cell becomes its bottom-right corner.
Let me test this by clicking a cell 2 cells away from a square to see if it still moves.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Test range: click 2 cells away from square 1 at (33-34, 15-16)
# Click at (32, 14) - 2 cells up-left from top-left corner (33, 15)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 32, 'col': 14}])
print(f"Click 2 cells away: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nM squares:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  M: {n['boundary']}")

# If that didn't work, try clicking adjacent to square 1
# Square 1 is at (33-34, 15-16)
# Click at (32, 15) - 1 cell above top-left
result2 = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 32, 'col': 15}])
print(f"\nClick 1 cell above: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nM squares after:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  M: {n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
